metadata.title: Exposure-Response Relationships — Study Design, Data Analysis, and Regulatory Applications

rules.rule_text: A drug can be determined to be safe and effective only when the relationship of beneficial and adverse effects to a defined exposure is known.
rules.source_section: Section II. Background
rules.verbatim_quote: Exposure-response information is at the heart of any determination of the safety and effectiveness of drugs. That is, a drug can be determined to be safe and effective only when the relationship of beneficial and adverse effects to a defined exposure is known.

rules.rule_text: In most cases, it is important to develop information on population exposure-response relationships for favorable and unfavorable effects, and information on how, and whether, exposure can be adjusted for various subsets of the population.
rules.source_section: Section II. Background
rules.verbatim_quote: In most cases, however, it is important to develop information on population exposure-response relationships for favorable and unfavorable effects, and information on how, and whether, exposure can be adjusted for various subsets of the population.
rules.exceptions: Some situations involving a very well-tolerated drug with little dose-related toxicity may not require this.

rules.rule_text: Critical exposure-response studies designed to provide primary evidence of efficacy must have prospectively defined hypotheses/objectives, use an appropriate control group, use randomization to ensure comparability of treatment groups and minimize bias, use well-defined and reliable methods for assessing response variables, and use other techniques to minimize bias.
rules.source_section: Section III.B. Information to Support a Determination of Safety and Efficacy
rules.verbatim_quote: Thus, we recommend that critical studies (1) have prospectively defined hypotheses/objectives, (2) use an appropriate control group, (3) use randomization to ensure comparability of treatment groups and to minimize bias, (4) use well-defined and reliable methods for assessing response variables, and (5) use other techniques to minimize bias.

rules.rule_text: A dose-response study is one kind of adequate and well-controlled trial that can provide primary clinical evidence of effectiveness. The dose-response study can help ensure that excessive doses (beyond those that add to efficacy) are not used, offering protection against unexpected and unrecognized dose-related toxicity.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.1. Contributing to Primary Evidence of Effectiveness and/or Safety
rules.verbatim_quote: A dose-response study is one kind of adequate and well-controlled trial that can provide primary clinical evidence of effectiveness. The dose-response study is a particularly informative design, allowing observations of benefits and risks at different doses and therefore providing an ability to weigh the benefits and risks when choosing doses. The dose-response study can help ensure that excessive doses (beyond those that add to efficacy) are not used, offering some protection against unexpected and unrecognized dose-related toxicity.

rules.rule_text: Systemic exposure data (e.g., plasma drug concentrations) are especially useful as part of dose-response studies when an assigned dose is poorly correlated with plasma concentrations, when there is large interindividual variability in pharmacokinetics, or when there is a nonlinear relationship between dose and plasma drug concentrations.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.1. Contributing to Primary Evidence of Effectiveness and/or Safety
rules.verbatim_quote: Systemic exposure data are especially useful when an assigned dose is poorly correlated with plasma concentrations, obscuring an existing concentration-response relationship. This can occur when there is a large degree of interindividual variability in pharmacokinetics or there is a nonlinear relationship between dose and plasma drug concentrations.

rules.rule_text: Exposure-response information can support dosage adjustments in patients where pharmacokinetic differences are expected because of intrinsic (demographic, underlying disease, genetic polymorphism) or extrinsic (diet, smoking, drug interactions) factors.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.3.b. Adjustment of dosages and dosing regimens in subpopulations
rules.verbatim_quote: Exposure-response information linking dose, concentration, and response can support dosage adjustments in patients where pharmacokinetic differences are expected or observed to occur because of one or more intrinsic (e.g., demographic, underlying or accompanying disease, genetic polymorphism) or extrinsic (e.g., diet, smoking, drug interactions) factors.

rules.rule_text: Exposure-response data are not likely to be useful in lieu of clinical data in supporting new dosing schedules unless the relationship of the measured responses to relevant safety and efficacy outcomes is well understood.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.3.c. New dose regimens, dosage forms and formulations, routes of administration
rules.verbatim_quote: Exposure-response data are not likely to be useful in lieu of clinical data in supporting new dosing schedules unless the relationship of the measured responses to relevant safety and efficacy outcomes is well understood.

rules.rule_text: Exposure-response data are not likely to obviate the need for clinical data when formulation or manufacturing changes result in altered pharmacokinetics, unless the relationships between measured responses and relevant clinical outcomes are well understood.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.3.c. New dose regimens, dosage forms and formulations, routes of administration
rules.verbatim_quote: Exposure-response data are not likely to obviate the need for clinical data when formulation or manufacturing changes result in altered pharmacokinetics, unless the relationships between measured responses and relevant clinical outcomes are well understood.

rules.rule_text: Exposure-response information can be obtained at steady state without consideration of the impact of fluctuations in exposure and response over time, or can be used to examine responses at various concentrations attained after a single dose during the dosing interval or over the course of treatment.
rules.source_section: Section IV. Dose-Concentration-Response Relationships and Effects Over Time
rules.verbatim_quote: Depending on the purpose of the study and the measurements made, exposure-response information can be obtained at steady state without consideration of the impact of fluctuations in exposure and response over time, or can be used to examine responses at the various concentrations attained after a single dose during the dosing interval or over the course of treatment.

rules.rule_text: Dose-response information can help identify an appropriate starting dose and determine the best way to adjust dosage for a particular patient. When the time course of response and the exposure-response relationship over time is assessed, time-related effects on drug action (induction, tolerance, and chronopharmacologic effects) can be detected.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A. Dose and Concentration-Time Relationships
rules.verbatim_quote: As noted in the ICH E4 guidance for industry on Dose-Response Information to Support Drug Registration, dose-response information can help identify an appropriate starting dose and determine the best way (how often and by how much) to adjust dosage for a particular patient. If the time course of response and the exposure-response relationship over time is also assessed, time-related effects on drug action (e.g., induction, tolerance, and chronopharmacologic effects) can be detected.

rules.rule_text: Concentration-response relationships obtained from nonrandomized data sets where patients are observed at observed concentrations can be misleading unless analyzed using specialized approaches and must be subjected to further evaluation due to potential confounding.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B. Concentration-Response Relationships: Two Approaches
rules.verbatim_quote: The first kind of study (# 1 above) is the usual or most common way of obtaining exposure-response information, but this kind of study can be misleading unless it is analyzed using specialized approaches (e.g., Sheiner, Hashimoto, and Beal 1991). Even when appropriately analyzed, potential confounding of the concentration-response relationship can occur and an observed concentration-response relationship may not be credible evidence of an exposure-response relationship.

rules.rule_text: When an exposure-response study is designed to support regulatory decisions by providing evidence of efficacy, randomization to exposure (dose or concentration) is critical.
rules.source_section: Section V.B. Exposure-Response Study Design
rules.verbatim_quote: When an exposure-response study is designed to support regulatory decisions by providing evidence of efficacy, randomization to exposure (dose or concentration) is critical.

rules.rule_text: It is important to examine the entire drug development database for potentially interesting exposure-response relationships, including sex differences in response that can sometimes be explained by observed sex-related PK data obtained during trials.
rules.source_section: Section V.B. Exposure-Response Study Design
rules.verbatim_quote: As emphasized in the ICH E4 guidance, it is important to examine the entire drug development database for potentially interesting exposure-response relationships. For example, sex differences in response can sometimes be explained by observed sex-related PK data obtained during trials (population PK data) or in studies obtaining blood samples for measuring plasma concentrations in patients with adverse effects.

rules.rule_text: To the extent possible, it is important that exposure-response studies include measurement of all active moieties (parent and active metabolites) that contribute significantly to the effects of the drug, especially when the route of administration is changed or when hepatic or renal impairment or concomitant drugs can alter the relative proportions of a drug and its active metabolites in plasma.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.1.a. Active moieties
rules.verbatim_quote: To the extent possible, it is important that exposure-response studies include measurement of all active moieties (parent and active metabolites) that contribute significantly to the effects of the drug. This is especially important when the route of administration of a drug is changed, as different routes of administration can result in different proportions of parent compound and metabolites in plasma. Similarly, hepatic or renal impairment or concomitant drugs can alter the relative proportions of a drug and its active metabolites in plasma.

rules.rule_text: Early elucidation of the PK and PD properties of individual enantiomers of optically active drugs is important in designing a dosing regimen and in deciding whether it can be of value to develop one of the pure enantiomers as the final drug product.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.1.b. Racemates and enantiomers
rules.verbatim_quote: Enantiomers sometimes differ in both their pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic properties. Early elucidation of the PK and PD properties of the individual enantiomers can help in designing a dosing regimen and in deciding whether it can be of value to develop one of the pure enantiomers as the final drug product.

rules.rule_text: For highly protein bound drugs, PK and PK-PD modeling based on unbound drug concentrations may be more informative, particularly if there is significant variation in binding among patients or in special populations.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.1.e. Unbound drug and/or active metabolite (protein binding)
rules.verbatim_quote: For highly protein bound drugs, PK and PK-PD modeling based on unbound drug concentrations may be more informative, particularly if there is significant variation in binding among patients or in special populations of patients.

rules.rule_text: The most appropriate representation of exposure will depend on the study objectives, the study design, and the nature of the relationship between exposure and response. Appropriate exposure metrics include AUC, Cmax, Cmin, or plasma concentration-time profiles.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.2. Exposure Variables
rules.verbatim_quote: The most appropriate representation of exposure will depend on the study objectives, the study design, and the nature of the relationship between exposure and response. If response varies substantially with time within a dosage interval, then the maximum information on exposure-response will normally be retrieved by relating response to concentration within the group and individual subjects. When a single pharmacodynamic response is obtained once on a given sampling day, it may be more appropriate to represent the exposure by more simplified metrics such as AUC, Cmax, or Cmin.

rules.rule_text: It is important to have a well-designed sampling plan for estimating peak plasma concentrations (Cmax) and to account for expected differences in PK profiles (e.g., in Tmax) due to demographics, disease states, and food effects.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.2.b. Peak plasma concentrations (Cmax)
rules.verbatim_quote: It is important to have a well-designed sampling plan for estimating peak concentrations and be able to account for expected differences in PK profiles (e.g., in Tmax, time to Cmax) due to demographics, disease states, and food effects, if any.

rules.rule_text: For sparse plasma concentration sampling in clinical trials, a specialized technique combining population PK analysis with Bayesian estimation method should be used to approximate population and individual PK parameters, providing an exposure variable that is more readily correlated to response.
rules.source_section: Section V.C.2.d. Sparse plasma concentrations
rules.verbatim_quote: In these circumstances, a specialized technique, population PK analysis combined with Bayesian estimation method, can be used to approximate population and individual PK parameters, providing an exposure variable that is more readily correlated to response than the sparse plasma concentrations themselves.

rules.rule_text: Both positive (efficacy) and negative (safety) effects of a drug must be characterized using a variety of measurements or response endpoints, including clinical outcomes, effects on a well-established surrogate, and effects on a more remote biomarker. In most cases, it is important to standardize the measurement of response endpoints across studies and between study sites and/or laboratories.
rules.source_section: Section V.D. Measuring Response
rules.verbatim_quote: Broadly speaking, both positive (efficacy) and negative (safety) effects of a drug can be characterized using a variety of measurements or response endpoints... In most cases, it is important to standardize the measurement of response endpoints across studies and between study sites and/or laboratories.

rules.rule_text: A biomarker is not considered an acceptable surrogate endpoint for a determination of efficacy of a new drug unless it has been empirically shown to function as a valid indicator of clinical benefit (i.e., is a valid surrogate). Theoretical justification alone does not meet the evidentiary standards for market access.
rules.source_section: Section V.D.1. Biomarkers
rules.verbatim_quote: From a regulatory perspective, a biomarker is not considered an acceptable surrogate endpoint for a determination of efficacy of a new drug unless it has been empirically shown to function as a valid indicator of clinical benefit (i.e., is a valid surrogate). Theoretical justification alone does not meet the evidentiary standards for market access.
rules.exceptions: FDA is able to rely on less well-established surrogates for accelerated approval of drugs that provide meaningful benefit over existing therapies for serious or life-threatening illnesses.

rules.rule_text: A surrogate endpoint is a laboratory measurement or physical sign used in therapeutic trials as a substitute for a clinically meaningful endpoint. A well-validated surrogate endpoint will predict the clinically meaningful endpoint of an intervention with consistent results in several settings.
rules.source_section: Section V.D.2. Surrogate Endpoint
rules.verbatim_quote: A surrogate endpoint is a laboratory measurement or physical sign used in therapeutic trials as a substitute for a clinically meaningful endpoint that is expected to reflect the effect of the therapy (Temple 1999). A well-validated surrogate endpoint will predict the clinically meaningful endpoint of an intervention (Lesko and Atkinson 2001), with consistent results in several settings.

rules.rule_text: Surrogates used as endpoints in trials will generally be unable to evaluate clinically relevant effects of the drug unrelated to the surrogate, whether these are beneficial or adverse.
rules.source_section: Section V.D.2. Surrogate Endpoint
rules.verbatim_quote: However, generally, in trials examining surrogate endpoints, even where the endpoint is well correlated with a clinical outcome, surrogates will be unable to evaluate clinically relevant effects of the drug unrelated to the surrogate, whether these are beneficial or adverse (Temple 1999).

rules.rule_text: In the process of PK-PD modeling, it is important to prospectively describe the statement of the problem (objectives, study design, available PK and PD data), the statement of assumptions, and the selection of the model.
rules.source_section: Section VI.B. Modeling Strategy
rules.verbatim_quote: In the process of PK-PD modeling, it is important to describe the following prospectively: [1. Statement of the Problem] The objectives of the modeling, the study design, and the available PK and PD data; [2. Statement of Assumptions] The assumptions of the model that can be related to dose-response, PK, PD, and/or one or more of the following...

rules.rule_text: The final selection of a PK-PD model will usually be based on the simplest model possible that has reasonable goodness of fit, and that provides a level of predictability appropriate for its use in decision making.
rules.source_section: Section VI.B.3. Selection of the Model
rules.verbatim_quote: The final selection of the model will usually be based on the simplest model possible that has reasonable goodness of fit, and that provides a level of predictability appropriate for its use in decision making.

rules.rule_text: We recommend that the predictive power of a model be dealt with during the study design as well as in the data analysis stages and that the study be designed to yield a predictive model. Model predictability is especially important when the model is intended to provide supportive evidence for primary efficacy studies, address safety issues, or support new doses and dosing regimens in new target populations.
rules.source_section: Section VI.B.4. Validation of the Model
rules.verbatim_quote: Generally, we recommend that the predictive power of a model be dealt with during the study design as well as in the data analysis stages and that the study be designed to yield a predictive model... The predictability is especially important when the model is intended to (1) provide supportive evidence for primary efficacy studies, (2) address safety issues, or (3) support new doses and dosing regimens in new target populations or subpopulations defined by intrinsic and extrinsic factors or when there is a change in dosage form and/or route of administration.

rules.rule_text: The exposure-response study report format and content should be based on the ICH E3 guidance on the Structure and Content of Clinical Study Reports, modified to include measurements of exposure and planned or actual modeling and simulation.
rules.source_section: Section VII. Submission Information: Exposure-Response Study Report
rules.verbatim_quote: It is advisable for the general format and content of a clinical study report to be based on that presented in the ICH E3 guidance on the Structure and Content of Clinical Study Reports, modified to include measurements of exposure and planned or actual modeling and simulation.

rules.rule_text: The exposure-response study report should include: the response variable and all covariate information; an explanation of how they were obtained; a description of the sampling design for PK and PD measures; a description of covariates including their distributions and measurement accuracy/precision; data quality control and editing procedures; and a detailed description of model building and reduction criteria.
rules.source_section: Section VII. Submission Information: Exposure-Response Study Report
rules.verbatim_quote: The report could also contain: The response variable and all covariate information; An explanation of how they were obtained; A description of the sampling design used to collect the PK and PD measures; A description of the covariates, including their distributions and, where appropriate, the accuracy and precision with which the responses were measured; Data quality control and editing procedures; A detailed description of the criteria and procedures for model building and reduction, including exploratory data analysis

rules.rule_text: The data analysis method description in the study report must include: (1) the chosen dose-response or PK-PD model, (2) assumptions and underlying rationale for model components, (3) the chosen model-fitting method, (4) a description of the treatment of outliers and missing data, and (5) diagrams of the analysis performed and representative control/command files.
rules.source_section: Section VII. Submission Information: Exposure-Response Study Report
rules.verbatim_quote: The following components of the data analysis method used in the study would also ordinarily be described: (1) the chosen dose-response or PK-PD model, (2) the assumptions and underlying rationale for model components (e.g., parameterization, error models), (3) the chosen model-fitting method, (4) a description of the treatment of outliers and missing data, where applicable, and (5) diagrams, if possible, of the analysis performed and representative control/command files for each significant model building and/or reduction step.

rules.rule_text: A complete exposure-response study report must include a comprehensive statement of the rationale for model building and reduction procedures, interpretation of the results, impact of protocol violations, discussion and presentation of supporting graphs, and the ability of the model to predict performance.
rules.source_section: Section VII. Submission Information: Exposure-Response Study Report
rules.verbatim_quote: A complete report would include a comprehensive statement of the rationale for model building and reduction procedures, interpretation of the results, impact of protocol violations, discussion and presentation of supporting graphs, and the ability of the model to predict performance.

rules.rule_text: Whether the exposure-response analysis was performed as an add-on to a clinical study or as a stand-alone study, it is important that the original study protocol and amendments be included in the appendix of the report.
rules.source_section: Section VII. Submission Information: Exposure-Response Study Report
rules.verbatim_quote: Whether the analysis was performed as a result of an add-on to a clinical study or as a stand-alone exposure-response study, it is important that the original study protocol and amendments be included in the appendix.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors are encouraged to submit both the reports and data files with BLA or NDA submissions in electronic format. The data file formats for exposure-response and other clinical pharmacology data should follow FDA electronic submission standards.
rules.source_section: Section VII. Submission Information: Exposure-Response Study Report
rules.verbatim_quote: In the meantime, sponsors are encouraged to submit both the reports and data files with BLA or NDA submissions in electronic format. Until the details are included in an electronic BLA or NDA guidance document, sponsors can consult the clinical pharmacology and biopharmaceutics reviewer or team leader on the data sets to be provided and elements to be included in the data sets.

rules.rule_text: A PK-PD relationship or data from an exposure-response study can be used to support use of a previously approved drug in a new target patient population (such as pediatrics), where clinical response is expected to be similar to the adult population, based on good understanding of the pathophysiology of the disease. A decision tree illustrating this approach is provided in Appendix B.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.3.a. New target populations
rules.verbatim_quote: A PK-PD relationship or data from an exposure-response study can be used to support use of a previously approved drug in a new target patient population, such as a pediatric population, where the clinical response is expected to be similar to the adult population, based on a good understanding of the pathophysiology of the disease, but there is uncertainty as to the appropriate dose and plasma concentration.

rules.rule_text: Descriptive or empirical model-based analysis of exposure-response data does not necessarily establish causality or provide a mechanistic understanding of a drug's effect and would not ordinarily be a basis for approval of a new drug.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A. General Considerations
rules.verbatim_quote: Descriptive or empirical model-based analysis does not necessarily establish causality or provide a mechanistic understanding of a drug's effect and would not ordinarily be a basis for approval of a new drug.

rules.rule_text: It is helpful if an appendix is provided in the exposure-response study report containing the data set used in the dose-response or PK-PD analysis, the programming codes along with the printouts of the results of the final model, and any additional important plots.
rules.source_section: Section VII. Submission Information: Exposure-Response Study Report
rules.verbatim_quote: It is helpful if an appendix is provided containing the data set used in the dose-response or PK-PD analysis, the programming codes along with the printouts of the results of the final model, and any additional important plots.

rules.rule_text: When exposure-response information is used to support a new formulation that is unintentionally pharmacokinetically different from the formulation used in clinical trials, in cases where the change can be determined not to have any pharmacologic effects (e.g., no effect on unwanted immunogenicity), exposure-response information may allow appropriate use of the new product. In the case of bioequivalence failures, a sponsor may support use of a wider confidence interval or acceptance of a real difference in bioavailability or exposure if they can demonstrate this would not lead to a therapeutic difference.
rules.source_section: Section III.B.3.c. New dose regimens, dosage forms and formulations
rules.verbatim_quote: It is possible to demonstrate differences in exposure that are real but not clinically important, even when the 90% confidence interval for the bioequivalence measures fall within the standard of 80-125%. It is possible for these bioequivalence studies to fail to meet the standard bioequivalence acceptance intervals of 80-125%. Rather than reformulating the product or repeating the bioequivalence study, a sponsor may be able to support the view that use of a wider confidence interval or accepting a real difference in bioavailability or exposure would not lead to a therapeutic difference.
